questions.question: What structure is responsible for relaxation of the lower esophageal sphincter?
questions.explanation: The myenteric plexus (Auerbach's) is mainly responsible for relaxing the LES through the release of nitric oxide.

questions.question: What is the main inhibitory neurotransmitter of the lower esophageal sphincter?
questions.explanation: Nitric oxide is the main neurotransmitter that induces relaxation of the LES; its deficiency causes achalasia.

questions.question: What study confirms failure to relax the LES in achalasia?
questions.explanation: High-resolution esophageal manometry is the gold standard to demonstrate the absence of LES relaxation.

questions.question: What is the first-line treatment for LES relaxation failure?
questions.explanation: Endoscopic pneumatic dilation is the first-line treatment for achalasia, by breaking the fibers of the LES.

questions.question: What histological alteration is observed in the myenteric plexus in achalasia?
questions.explanation: In achalasia, there is a selective loss of inhibitory neurons in the myenteric plexus, which prevents relaxation of the LES.

questions.question: Which manometric finding is characteristic of achalasia?
questions.explanation: Achalasia is characterized by elevated basal LES pressure and lack of complete relaxation during swallowing.

questions.question: What is the main cause of esophageal achalasia?
questions.explanation: Achalasia is caused by degeneration of inhibitory neurons in the myenteric plexus, which prevents relaxation of the lower esophageal sphincter and impairs peristalsis.

questions.question: What test is the gold standard for diagnosing achalasia?
questions.explanation: High-resolution esophageal manometry is the gold standard, as it demonstrates the absence of peristalsis and lack of relaxation of the lower esophageal sphincter.

questions.question: What is the first-line treatment in achalasia type II?
questions.explanation: Heller myotomy with partial fundoplication is the first-line surgical treatment for achalasia, with excellent success rates and lower recurrence.

questions.question: What radiological finding is characteristic of achalasia?
questions.explanation: The bird's beak sign is seen on the barium esophagram, due to distal narrowing of the esophagus due to the unrelaxed sphincter and proximal dilation.

questions.question: What complication is most feared in long-standing achalasia?
questions.explanation: Chronic achalasia increases the risk of esophageal squamous cell carcinoma due to prolonged food stasis and chronic mucosal inflammation.

questions.question: Which subtype of achalasia presents the best response to pneumatic dilation?
questions.explanation: Achalasia type II, characterized by panesophageal compression, has the best response to pneumatic dilation and surgical treatment.

questions.question: What is the main pathophysiological mechanism of achalasia that causes progressive dysphagia for solids and liquids?
questions.explanation: In achalasia there is a degeneration of the myenteric plexuses that prevents the relaxation of the lower esophageal sphincter (LES), generating functional obstruction and dysphagia for both solids and liquids.

questions.question: Which imaging study is considered first-line for the initial diagnosis of achalasia?
questions.explanation: The barium esophagram is the initial study that in achalasia shows esophageal dilation with distal narrowing in a 'bird's beak', a characteristic finding that guides the diagnosis.

questions.question: What is the gold standard to confirm the diagnosis of achalasia?
questions.explanation: High-resolution esophageal manometry is the gold standard for confirming achalasia, demonstrating aperistalsis of the esophageal body and incomplete relaxation of the LES with elevated pressure.

questions.question: What is the first-line endoscopic treatment for achalasia?
questions.explanation: Endoscopic pneumatic dilation is the first-line endoscopic treatment in achalasia, with proven efficacy in breaking LES fibers and improving progressive dysphagia.

questions.question: Which subtype of achalasia is associated with a greater response to pneumatic dilation?
questions.explanation: Achalasia type II, characterized by panesophageal compression on manometry, presents the best response to pneumatic dilation, with success rates greater than 90%.

questions.question: What late complication can a patient with untreated achalasia develop?
questions.explanation: Chronic food stasis and inflammation in untreated achalasia increases the risk of esophageal squamous cell carcinoma, with an incidence up to 50 times higher than the general population.

questions.question: What is the main cause of regurgitation in achalasia?
options: Hypertrophy of the upper esophageal sphincter
Hypotonia of the lower esophageal sphincter
Failure to relax the lower esophageal sphincter
questions.explanation: In achalasia, regurgitation is due to the lack of relaxation of the lower esophageal sphincter (LES) and the absence of peristalsis, which prevents the passage of food to the stomach.

questions.question: What characterizes the typical regurgitation of achalasia?
questions.explanation: Regurgitation in achalasia consists of undigested food mixed with saliva, without bile, due to functional obstruction at the level of the lower esophageal sphincter.

questions.question: Which position typically worsens regurgitation in achalasia?
questions.explanation: The supine position encourages food retained in the esophagus to rise due to gravity, increasing regurgitation and the risk of aspiration in patients with achalasia.

questions.question: What complication is most frequently associated with chronic regurgitation in achalasia?
questions.explanation: Chronic regurgitation of undigested food in achalasia predisposes to pulmonary aspiration, causing chemical pneumonitis or secondary bacterial pneumonia.

questions.question: What study confirms the diagnosis of achalasia in persistent regurgitation?
questions.explanation: Esophageal manometry is the gold standard for diagnosing achalasia, showing aperistalsis and failure to relax the lower esophageal sphincter in patients with regurgitation.

questions.question: What is the first-line treatment for regurgitation in achalasia?
questions.explanation: Pneumatic dilation of the lower esophageal sphincter is an effective first-line treatment to relieve functional obstruction and reduce regurgitation in achalasia.

questions.question: What is the test of choice to confirm achalasia?
questions.explanation: Esophageal manometry is the gold standard for the diagnosis of achalasia, as it measures lower esophageal sphincter pressure and esophageal motility.

questions.question: Which manometric finding is characteristic of achalasia?
questions.explanation: In achalasia, manometry shows absence of esophageal peristalsis (aperistalsis) and lack of relaxation of the lower esophageal sphincter.

questions.question: What parameter does esophageal manometry evaluate in achalasia?
questions.explanation: Manometry evaluates the pressure of the lower esophageal sphincter (LES) and relaxation during swallowing, key to diagnosing achalasia.

questions.question: What is the main differential diagnosis of achalasia?
questions.explanation: Esophageal carcinoma (pseudoachalasia) is the main differential diagnosis, as it can mimic the manometric findings of idiopathic achalasia.

questions.question: What type of manometry is preferred for achalasia?
questions.explanation: High-resolution esophageal manometry (HRM) is the preferred technique as it provides a detailed topographic representation of esophageal motility.

questions.question: What manometric classification is used in achalasia?
questions.explanation: The Chicago classification (based on high-resolution manometry) categorizes achalasia into three types based on the pattern of pressures and peristalsis.

questions.question: What manometric finding defines achalasia type I?
questions.explanation: Achalasia type I is characterized by complete esophageal aperistalsis without contractions in the esophageal body, while LES presents incomplete relaxation.

questions.question: What is the central pathophysiological mechanism of aperistalsis in achalasia?
options: Loss of inhibitory neurons in the myenteric plexus
Diffuse spasm of the distal esophagus
questions.explanation: The loss of inhibitory neurons (nitric oxide, VIP) in the myenteric plexus causes aperistalsis and lack of relaxation of the LES, characteristic of achalasia.

questions.question: What study confirms esophageal aperistalsis in suspected achalasia?
questions.explanation: High-resolution esophageal manometry is the gold standard for diagnosing achalasia by demonstrating aperistalsis and impaired relaxation of the LES.

questions.question: What pressure pattern is seen in the esophageal body during swallowing in achalasia?
questions.explanation: In achalasia, the esophageal body shows aperistalsis with elevated basal pressure (esophagorrhagia) due to lack of relaxation and food retention.

questions.question: What is the first-line treatment for achalasia with aperistalsis?
questions.explanation: Laparoscopic Heller myotomy is the first-line surgical treatment for achalasia, as it cuts the LES and allows esophageal emptying.

questions.question: Which subtype of achalasia is associated with a complete absence of contractions in the esophageal body?
questions.explanation: Achalasia type I is defined by complete aperistalsis without contractions in the esophageal body, while type II presents panesophageal compression and type III spastic contractions.

questions.question: What pathophysiological mechanism explains the elevated LES pressure at rest in achalasia?
questions.explanation: Loss of inhibitory neurons (nitric oxide and VIP) in the myenteric plexus of the LES causes lack of relaxation and elevated resting pressure in achalasia.

questions.question: What is the manometric value that defines elevated LES pressure at rest in achalasia?
questions.explanation: In esophageal manometry, a resting LES pressure greater than 45 mmHg is considered elevated and is characteristic of achalasia.

questions.question: What is the esophageal motor disorder typically associated with elevated LES pressure at rest?
questions.explanation: Achalasia type I is characterized by elevated LES pressure at rest and absence of esophageal peristalsis, being the classic associated disorder.

questions.question: What imaging study can show a hypertrophic and unrelaxed LES in achalasia?
questions.explanation: The barium esophagram shows the mouse tail sign and contrast retention, evidencing an LES that does not relax adequately.

questions.question: What is the first-line endoscopic treatment for elevated LES pressure in achalasia?
questions.explanation: Pneumatic balloon dilation is the first-line endoscopic treatment to reduce elevated LES pressure in achalasia, with a high initial success rate.

questions.question: What drug is used to reduce LES pressure in achalasia as a temporary alternative?
questions.explanation: Nifedipine, a calcium channel blocker, reduces LES pressure by relaxing smooth muscle, used as a temporary palliative treatment in achalasia.

questions.question: What radiological finding is characteristic of achalasia on an esophagram?
questions.explanation: The bird's beak sign is the classic esophagram image of achalasia, showing distal narrowing of the esophagus with proximal dilation due to lack of relaxation of the lower esophageal sphincter.

questions.question: What is the underlying pathophysiology that produces the bird's beak sign?
options: Hypertonia of the lower esophageal sphincter
Achalasia of the upper esophageal sphincter
questions.explanation: The bird's beak sign is due to hypertonia and lack of relaxation of the lower esophageal sphincter (LES), which prevents the passage of barium and generates a distal conical stenosis with proximal dilation of the esophagus.

questions.question: What imaging study is used to identify the bird's beak sign?
questions.explanation: The barium esophagram (esophagogastroduodenal series) is the radiological study that classically shows the bird's beak sign, characterized by a distal narrowing of the esophagus with proximal dilation.

questions.question: What other diagnostic method confirms achalasia after seeing the bird's beak sign?
questions.explanation: Esophageal manometry is the gold standard for confirming achalasia, showing aperistalsis of the esophageal body and failure to relax the lower esophageal sphincter, findings that correlate with the bird's beak sign.

questions.question: What is the first-line treatment for achalasia with bird's beak sign?
questions.explanation: Laparoscopic Heller myotomy is the first-line surgical treatment for achalasia, as it cuts the fibers of the lower esophageal sphincter, relieving the functional obstruction that produces the bird's beak sign.

questions.question: What complication can be associated with untreated achalasia with bird's beak sign?
questions.explanation: Untreated chronic achalasia increases the risk of esophageal squamous cell carcinoma due to stasis and chronic inflammation of the dilated esophagus, a serious complication associated with the bird's beak sign.

questions.question: What is the characteristic finding on the esophagram of a patient with achalasia?
questions.explanation: In achalasia, the esophagram typically shows dilation of the proximal esophagus due to lack of relaxation of the lower esophageal sphincter, with distal bird-beak narrowing.

questions.question: What anatomical structure is primarily evaluated by esophagram in achalasia?
questions.explanation: The esophagram in achalasia evaluates the morphology of the distal esophagus and the lower esophageal sphincter, which shows a bird's beak stenosis due to lack of relaxation.

questions.question: What is the first-line treatment for achalasia when there is proximal esophageal dilation?
questions.explanation: Laparoscopic Heller myotomy is the surgical treatment of choice for achalasia, as it cuts the fibers of the lower esophageal sphincter, relieving functional obstruction and proximal dilation.

questions.question: What complication can be observed in the esophagram of advanced achalasia?
questions.explanation: In advanced achalasia, progressive proximal esophageal dilation can lead to esophageal megasigma, a massive, tortuous dilation of the esophagus visible on the esophagram.

questions.question: What complementary test confirms the diagnosis of achalasia in the face of a suggestive esophagram?
questions.explanation: High-resolution esophageal manometry is the gold standard for confirming achalasia, demonstrating aperistalsis of the esophageal body and lack of relaxation of the lower esophageal sphincter.

questions.question: What is the main pathophysiological mechanism of proximal esophageal dilation in achalasia?
options: Hypertonia of the upper esophageal sphincter
Mechanical obstruction due to distal tumor
questions.explanation: In achalasia, degeneration of the myenteric plexuses causes aperistalsis of the esophageal body, which prevents the transit of the food bolus and generates proximal dilation of the esophagus.

questions.question: What is the recommended initial treatment for esophageal achalasia?
questions.explanation: Pneumatic dilation is a first-line option in the treatment of achalasia, as it breaks the fibers of the lower esophageal sphincter, improving esophageal emptying.

questions.question: What surgical procedure is an alternative to pneumatic dilation in achalasia?
questions.explanation: Heller myotomy, usually combined with partial fundoplication, is the standard surgery for achalasia, offering an effective alternative to pneumatic dilation.

questions.question: What is the mechanism of action of pneumatic dilation in achalasia?
questions.explanation: Pneumatic dilation acts by inflating a balloon that mechanically breaks the muscle fibers of the lower esophageal sphincter, reducing resting pressure.

questions.question: What factor favors the choice of myotomy over pneumatic dilation?
questions.explanation: In young patients (<40 years), Heller myotomy is usually preferred due to its greater durability and lower need for retreatment compared to pneumatic dilation.

questions.question: What is the most feared complication of pneumatic dilation?
questions.explanation: Esophageal perforation is the most serious complication of pneumatic dilation, with an incidence of 1-5%, requiring urgent surgical management in most cases.

questions.question: What study confirms the diagnosis before treatment of achalasia?
questions.explanation: High-resolution esophageal manometry is the diagnostic standard for achalasia, showing aperistalsis and failure to relax the lower esophageal sphincter.

questions.question: What is the main objective of Heller myotomy in achalasia?
options: Resect the distal third of the esophagus
Dilate the cardia with a balloon
questions.explanation: Heller myotomy consists of the surgical cutting of the fibers of the lower esophageal sphincter to reduce pressure and allow the passage of the bolus in patients with achalasia.

questions.question: Which of the following is a common surgical approach for Heller myotomy?
questions.explanation: Currently, Heller myotomy is preferably performed laparoscopically, as it offers less morbidity, better visualization, and faster recovery than open surgery.

questions.question: What procedure is usually associated with Heller myotomy to prevent gastroesophageal reflux?
questions.explanation: Partial fundoplication (generally anterior Dor type) is added to Heller myotomy to reduce the risk of postoperative acid reflux, without excessively increasing resistance to the passage of food.

questions.question: What is the main indication for Heller myotomy in achalasia?
options: Achalasia in patients with severe coagulopathy
Achalasia type I non-responsive to botulinum toxin
questions.explanation: Heller myotomy is indicated in patients with achalasia who do not respond to endoscopic treatments such as botulinum toxin or pneumatic dilation, especially in types I and II.

questions.question: Which immediate postoperative complication is most specific to Heller myotomy?
questions.explanation: Esophageal perforation is the most feared and specific complication of Heller myotomy, since it occurs due to inadvertent dissection of the mucosa during section of the muscle fibers of the distal esophagus.

questions.question: What is the reported long-term success rate for laparoscopic Heller myotomy in achalasia?
questions.explanation: Laparoscopic Heller myotomy has a long-term (5-10 years) clinical success rate of 85-95%, with sustained improvement in dysphagia and a low reintervention rate in centers with experience.

questions.question: What is the main risk factor for developing squamous cell carcinoma of the esophagus in achalasia?
questions.explanation: In achalasia, failure to relax the lower esophageal sphincter causes prolonged stasis of food and secretions, leading to chronic inflammation and epithelial metaplasia, increasing the risk of squamous cell carcinoma.

questions.question: What complication is associated with massive dilation of the esophagus in long-standing achalasia?
questions.explanation: Untreated or long-standing achalasia produces progressive dilation of the esophagus, called megaesophagus, which can reach diameters greater than 6 cm and is associated with a higher risk of squamous cell carcinoma.

questions.question: What is the approximate incidence of squamous cell carcinoma in patients with long-standing achalasia?
questions.explanation: Epidemiological studies report that the risk of squamous cell carcinoma in achalasia is 0.5% to 1%, with an increase of 16 to 33 times compared to the general population, especially after 10-15 years of evolution.

questions.question: What histological type of cancer is most common in achalasia?
questions.explanation: In achalasia, squamous cell carcinoma is the most common histological type, due to chronic irritation of the esophageal mucosa due to food stasis, unlike adenocarcinoma, which is more associated with gastroesophageal reflux.

questions.question: Which of the following factors is associated with a higher risk of squamous cell carcinoma in achalasia?
options: Duration of the disease greater than 10 years
questions.explanation: The risk of squamous cell carcinoma in achalasia increases significantly after 10-15 years of disease progression, regardless of the treatment performed, which is why periodic endoscopic surveillance is recommended.

questions.question: What is the recommendation for endoscopic surveillance in patients with long-standing achalasia?
questions.explanation: Endoscopic surveillance is recommended every 3-5 years in patients with achalasia of more than 10-15 years of evolution, especially in those with megaesophagus, to detect dysplasia or squamous cell carcinoma in early stages.
